finding: ATM validation surfaces can grow monotonically and make common flows slower because validators do not yet emit enough per-validator usage, latency, trigger-rate, failure-rate, and defect-interception counters to justify their placement in fast, default, full, or archive tiers.
expectedBehavior: Every validation unit should emit lightweight instrument-mode counters: validator id, mode tier, invocation count, skipped count, duration, failure count, blocking count, downstream defect/incident link when known, and task/surface context. Fast/default/full placement should be evidence-driven. Rarely used or expensive validators should move to full; never-used or no-impact validators should be archived or sampled.
evidenceOrRepro: During 2026-07-19 dogfood, repeated source and governance commits required increasingly broad validation. The user observed that validation will keep getting slower unless each validator has counters that prove frequency and value. Existing validate:cli fast reports aggregate timing, but not enough per-validator lifecycle data to demote or retire specific checks.
followUp: Add a validator telemetry schema and runtime journal that is fail-open and gitignored by default, with digest rollups in close/batch evidence. Integrate it into validate:cli, doctor, pre-commit, task audit, runner-sync, and batch checkpoint surfaces. Add policy thresholds: promote high-value frequent blockers to fast/default, demote low-frequency or high-cost checks to full, archive never-used/no-impact validators after a defined observation window, and require validation telemetry itself to prove it drove at least one optimization decision or be sampled.
